mcqs.question: What is the primary architectural difference between CPUs and GPUs?

options.text: CPUs have many simple cores while GPUs have few complex cores
CPUs have few complex cores while GPUs have many simple cores
CPUs are optimized for parallel processing while GPUs are optimized for sequential processing
There is no significant architectural difference between them

mcqs.explanation: CPUs are designed with few complex cores optimized for sequential processing and complex control logic, while GPUs have many simple cores optimized for data-parallel computations.

mcqs.question: Which memory type in GPU architecture is fastest but has the smallest capacity?

options.text: Global memory
Shared memory
Constant memory
Registers

mcqs.explanation: Registers are the fastest memory type in GPU architecture but have the smallest capacity and are private to each thread.

mcqs.question: According to Amdahl's Law, if 80% of a program can be parallelized and we use 8 processors, what is the maximum theoretical speedup?
What is the purpose of the '<<< >>>' syntax in CUDA programming?

options.text: It defines a special GPU data type
It specifies the kernel launch configuration (grid and block dimensions)
It indicates a GPU memory allocation operation
It marks the beginning and end of a GPU kernel function

mcqs.explanation: The '<<< >>>' syntax in CUDA specifies the execution configuration for a kernel launch, including the number of blocks and threads per block.

mcqs.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'thread divergence' in GPU programming?

options.text: Threads executing different instructions simultaneously within a warp
Threads accessing memory in non-coalesced patterns
Threads from different blocks communicating with each other
Threads executing the same instruction on different data elements

mcqs.explanation: Thread divergence occurs when threads within a warp (group of threads that execute together) take different execution paths, leading to serialized execution and reduced performance.
